lens.included_activities: United States lower-middle-market trade, professional, scholarly, newsletter, and paid specialist periodical publishers with recurring subscriber, advertiser, sponsor, licensing, data, event, or institutional relationships and transferable editorial brands and operations.
lens.excluded_activities: Mass consumer entertainment and comic-only publishing, captive member publications not classified as publishers, one-off publications, printing-only operations, shells, inactive titles, non-control financing vehicles, and owner-personality publications that cannot continue after a control transfer.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Readers, professionals, institutions, libraries, and enterprises buy recurring subscriptions, access, licenses, or publication services; advertisers and sponsors buy recurring access to defined audiences; some publishers add events, research, data, custom content, and lead-generation services. The publisher controls the editorial product in print, electronic, or internet-only form.
lens.deviation_from_default: NAICS 513120 mixes consumer magazines, comics, scholarly journals, newsletters, and trade journals whose demand, customer, and accountability structures are too different for one coherent screen. The lens is narrowed to trade, professional, scholarly, newsletter, and paid specialist publishers with recurring external commercial relationships; mass consumer entertainment and comic-only publishing are excluded for coherence, not attractiveness.

a.bridge: BLS measures occupations and wages for the broader NAICS 513100 publishing group, which combines newspapers, periodicals, books, and directories. Its large editorial, media, sales, business, and administrative workforce is mapped to the narrowed periodical lens using BLS descriptions of editor and journalist tasks. Research, transcription, drafting, rewriting, proofreading, fact-check support, metadata, layout, archive retrieval, audience segmentation, ad preparation, customer service, and routine format conversion are treated as exposed; expert commissioning, peer or editorial judgment, source relationships, original interviews, art direction, final approval, and accountable publication remain human-led. Periodicals may have more repeatable text workflows than newspapers, raising exposure, but scholarly and specialist accuracy requirements can lower substitution.
a.rationale: Recurring publication cycles generate large volumes of text, image, metadata, audience, marketing, subscription, and administrative work. AI can assist article research, abstracts, first drafts, copy editing, tagging, personalization, newsletter production, ad proposals, and archive use. Specialist expertise, author and reviewer relationships, verification, taste, brand voice, rights clearance, and final editorial accountability constrain substitution.
a.change_evidence: A six-digit wage-weighted time study of trade, scholarly, newsletter, and specialist periodical staff separating already automated from remaining editorial, audience, sales, subscription, production, and administrative tasks would materially change the range.
caveats: The occupational evidence combines periodicals with newspapers, books, and directories.
Published occupation shares do not directly measure wage-weighted task exposure or current automation.
The narrowed subsegments differ in peer review, specialist expertise, design intensity, and advertising operations.
The frozen labor-share input is an ancestor-sector estimate and may not represent periodical economics.

rho.bridge: The Reuters Institute's survey of 326 news executives across 51 countries found 63 percent saying newsrooms were being transformed somewhat and 24 percent fully by generative AI. This is an international news-publisher perception measure, not implemented task coverage in US LMM trade, scholarly, or specialist periodicals. It is translated downward because small publishers face fragmented systems, rights and author-policy constraints, specialist accuracy needs, customer confidentiality, limited integration staff, hallucination risk, and continued human review. The source may overstate implementation through executive selection and news-industry urgency, while embedded AI in common editorial and marketing tools may make adoption faster.
rho.rationale: AI can enter through content-management, office, marketing, analytics, transcription, and customer-support tools without a full platform rebuild. Implementation is constrained where expert authorship, peer review, source provenance, copyright, advertiser separation, specialist accuracy, and subscriber trust are central to the product.
rho.change_evidence: A representative US periodical-publisher survey linking deployed tools to workflow coverage, staff hours, avoided hires, author and reviewer policies, correction rates, rights incidents, subscription outcomes, and total technology cost by subsegment and size would materially change the estimate.
caveats: The adoption survey covers international news organizations, not the narrowed US periodical lens.
Perceived transformation does not measure implemented substitution of exposed labor.
Scholarly, trade, newsletter, and specialist publishers have different approval and rights constraints.

e.rationale: The official definition spans magazine, comic, scholarly-journal, newsletter, and trade-journal publishers and describes owned-content production plus advertising. Eligibility is concentrated in the narrowed trade, professional, scholarly, newsletter, and specialist segment where recurring subscriptions, institutional access, sponsorship, data, events, custom content, or lead-generation relationships resemble repeat external services and where brands, archives, audiences, contracts, and workflows can transfer. Consumer-only titles, thin owner-personality brands, captive or subsidized publications, inactive titles, and firms without normalized LMM economics are excluded.
e.change_evidence: A 513120 firm census linking title type, subscriber and advertiser mix, institutional contracts, recurring services, normalized earnings, ownership, key-person dependence, title activity, and post-transfer continuity would materially change the share.
caveats: No direct audit measures the share of LMM 513120 firms in the narrowed recurring-service subsegments.
A recurring subscription to an owned content product is not automatically an outsourced service.
The frozen dataset declares no defensible n-band value, so the eligible-firm count will remain missing.
The estimate is sensitive to how scholarly publishing and paid specialist information are treated under the lens.

s5.rationale: Trade and specialist titles can be acquired for subscriber lists, brands, archives, events, advertiser relationships, and adjacent information products, while founder succession and print pressure can motivate sales. Qualifying transfers remain limited by owner-editor dependence, uncertain audience durability, rights and contributor contracts, nonprofit or institutional ownership, distressed closures, and the absence of a transaction denominator for eligible US LMM periodical firms.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year database of 513120 control transfers and failed sales matched to legal entities, title portfolios, enterprise value, normalized earnings, buyer type, control conveyed, owner age, closures, and post-close subscriber retention would materially change the probability.
caveats: No defensible transaction-rate denominator for eligible LMM periodical publishers was found.
Title licensing and operating agreements may change publishers without transferring the underlying firm.
Multi-title portfolio deals can overstate firm-level opportunity, while private single-title deals may be missed.

q.rationale: Fixed subscriptions, institutional licenses, sponsorship packages, advertising commitments, event contracts, and data products generally do not reprice automatically when editorial or administrative cost falls, enabling initial retention. Over five years, customers can demand lower prices or more output, advertisers can reallocate on performance, authors and reviewers may require safeguards, and competition from free content, platforms, and lower-cost entrants can share away the benefit. Software, rights, verification, and product reinvestment also reduce net retention.
q.change_evidence: Contract- and publisher-level panels linking AI deployment to subscription and license pricing, churn, advertising yield, content volume, contributor cost, software and review expense, event economics, and normalized cash flow through renewals would materially change the retained share.
caveats: No direct study measures five-year post-AI benefit retention in LMM periodical publishing.
Advertising, institutional licenses, subscriptions, publication charges, events, and data products have different pass-through dynamics.
Savings may be reinvested in content quality and audience acquisition rather than retained as cash.

d5.bridge: BLS reports periodical-publisher output declining 1.5 percent annually over 1987-2022, which compounds to roughly 0.93 over five years if continued. Its broader 513100 matrix projects employment declining 8.9 percent from 2024 to 2034. These historical output and broader employment measures are translated into the year-five constant-price demand ratio for the narrowed periodical service basket. Long-run print-heavy history may overstate contraction for paid digital, scholarly, and B2B products, while platform substitution, search loss, and consumer-magazine weakness can understate downside.
d5.rationale: Specialist professional information, scholarly communication, newsletters, community and trade identity, advertising access, and recurring institutional use support durable demand. Print migration, fragmented attention, free alternatives, creator media, platform disintermediation, search changes, and reduced advertising constrain the current basket, while digital subscriptions, events, data, licensing, and niche authority provide offsets.
d5.change_evidence: A five-year subsegment forecast combining inflation-adjusted subscription, institutional-license, advertising, sponsorship, event, data, and publication revenue with paid accounts, churn, title starts and closures, engagement, issue frequency, and platform referrals would materially change the multiplier.
caveats: Historical output and employment are not direct measures of constant-quality demand.
The 1987-2022 periodical series is print-heavy and may not represent the next five years.
The narrowed trade, scholarly, newsletter, and specialist segment may be more durable than aggregate periodical publishing.
The range permits modest growth but also material contraction.

o.rationale: An accountable periodical operator remains important for commissioning, author and reviewer networks, expert validation, editorial selection, rights, corrections, brand standards, advertiser separation, audience curation, archives, and institutional trust. Commodity summaries, listicles, basic newsletters, layout, aggregation, and some discovery can be self-served by creators, associations, platforms, databases, or software, reducing the operator-required share.
o.change_evidence: Subsegment substitution studies comparing operator-led periodicals with creator-, association-, platform-, database-, or AI-supplied alternatives on accuracy, renewal, usage, citation, advertiser outcomes, rights compliance, and total cost would materially change the share.
caveats: No direct study isolates operator substitution in NAICS 513120.
Scholarly and technical journals require more accountable curation than commodity consumer content.
Demand for information can persist while the traditional periodical operator loses distribution and monetization.

narrative.executive_view: The narrowed trade, scholarly, newsletter, and specialist periodical segment has a large AI-assistable editorial and commercial workflow base and recurring customer relationships, but exact eligible-firm and transfer denominators are absent and demand continues to migrate toward platforms, creators, and digital formats.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can assist research, abstracts, transcription, first drafts, copy editing, metadata, tagging, newsletters, personalization, archive retrieval, ad proposals, customer service, and multi-format production. Expert commissioning, peer or editorial judgment, author relationships, verification, rights, brand voice, and final approval remain accountable human work.
narrative.value_capture: Subscriptions, licenses, sponsorships, and fixed advertising packages can retain near-term savings, while renewals, performance pricing, contributor safeguards, software costs, free alternatives, and reinvestment in differentiated content share away benefits over time.
narrative.firm_availability: Transferable value can reside in specialist brands, archives, subscriber files, author networks, advertiser relationships, events, and data products. Confidence is limited by the missing LMM denominator, no clean transaction-rate series, owner-editor dependence, licensing structures, and multi-title portfolio deals.
narrative.demand_durability: Professional, scholarly, and niche information remains useful, but aggregate periodical output has contracted and traditional formats face fragmented attention, free content, creator competition, search change, and advertising pressure. Paid digital, institutional use, events, data, and niche authority can offset part of the decline.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: Key risks are lens sensitivity across heterogeneous subsegments, no defensible n-band, no direct transfer denominator, overstated labor substitution, AI accuracy and rights failures, platform and search disintermediation, advertiser concentration, subscriber churn, owner dependence, and content commoditization.
narrative.principal_driver: Repeat editorial, audience, subscription, advertising, and administrative workflows are unusually amenable to AI assistance.
narrative.principal_weakness: Missing firm and transfer denominators combine with heterogeneous business models and ongoing distribution substitution.

sources.title: 2022 NAICS Manual: 513120 Periodical Publishers
sources.supports: Official definition covering magazine, scholarly-journal, newsletter, trade-journal, and comic publishers; editorial production, advertising, and print or electronic formats.

sources.title: May 2023 OEWS: NAICS 513100 Newspaper, Periodical, Book, and Directory Publishers
sources.supports: Broader publishing-industry occupational employment and wage mix, including editorial, media, sales, business, administrative, technology, and production work.

sources.title: Occupational Outlook Handbook: Editors
sources.supports: Editor tasks in newspapers and periodicals, including correction, rewriting, fact verification, content selection, layout, research, contracts, and final approval.

sources.title: Occupational Outlook Handbook: News Analysts, Reporters, and Journalists
sources.supports: Research, source development, interviews, analysis, writing, updating, multimedia, and audience duties for newspaper, magazine, and website publishers.

sources.title: Journalism, Media, and Technology Trends and Predictions 2025
sources.supports: International publisher-executive evidence on generative-AI transformation, licensing expectations, and platform strategy.

sources.title: Public Attitudes Towards the Use of AI in Journalism
sources.supports: Audience acceptance is greater for backend and assistive AI than for automated content, supporting continued human review and accountability.

sources.title: Annual Percent Change in Hours and Output, and Current Employment Level
sources.supports: Long-run 1987-2022 periodical-publisher hours decline of 2.0 percent annually and output decline of 1.5 percent annually.

sources.title: National Employment Matrix: NAICS 513100, 2024-2034
sources.supports: Broader publishing employment mix and projected 8.9 percent ten-year employment decline, including detailed editorial, media, sales, administrative, technology, and production occupations.
